ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for purple rice yoghurt. FOODON_03315671 (yogurt food product) is used as the closest defensible parent class. Purple rice component is not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Yoghurt, plain) is the closest defensible anchor. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for purple rice yoghurt as a composite dish. Purple rice grain component is not separately represented in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for purple rice yoghurt composite dish. SNOMED CT coverage for composite Asian grain-dairy dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Original Purple Rice Yoghurt is approximately 150 g (one cup), providing around 246 kcal, 9.8 g protein, 6.3 g fat, and 37.8 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Original Purple Rice Yoghurt — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Original Purple Rice Yoghurt (cr_id G00010445). All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients are not reported in the HPB entry and are set to -1 where unavailable.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for black glutinous rice / purple rice
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Original Purple Rice Yoghurt as a composite dish. GI estimated at 50 (low) based on two analogous approved-source anchors: (1) purple/black glutinous rice has a reported GI of approximately 42–55 in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (lower than white rice due to intact bran, anthocyanin content, and resistant starch); (2) plain yoghurt has a GI of approximately 14–36 (University of Sydney GI Database). The composite dish GI is estimated at 50, reflecting the dominant carbohydrate contribution of purple rice (25.19 g/100g total carbohydrate) moderated by the yoghurt matrix and dietary fibre (3.7 g/100g). Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (50 × 25.19 × 1.5) / 100 = 18.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g, partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g and dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 231 mg/100g is in the borderline range; individuals with hypertension should limit serving size to approximately 150 g and monitor cumulative daily sodium intake, while the favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio (0.71) and dietary fibre content support a net blood-pressure-lowering profile consistent with DASH diet principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 25.19 g/100g with low GI (~50) and dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g moderating glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Purple rice yoghurt has a low estimated GI of approximately 50 and a dietary fibre content of 3.7 g/100g that slows glucose absorption; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should manage portion size to approximately 150 g per serving to limit carbohydrate load to ~37.8 g, and should confirm the absence of added sweeteners in the yoghurt component.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low sugar (0.21 g/100g), moderate dietary fibre (3.7 g/100g), anthocyanins from purple rice, and cholesterol 0 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Purple rice yoghurt is cardioprotective in profile: cholesterol is 0 mg/100g, dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100g supports LDL reduction, and anthocyanins in purple rice have demonstrated anti-inflammatory and endothelial-protective effects in peer-reviewed literature; total fat at 4.21 g/100g is moderate and saturated fat is estimated to be low from the yoghurt component.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anthocyanins from purple rice and live cultures from yoghurt supporting gut-immune axis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Purple rice provides anthocyanins with potent anti-inflammatory antioxidant activity, and the yoghurt component contributes live bacterial cultures that support gut microbiome immune regulation; calcium at 54 mg/100g provides modest bone support relevant to RA patients on long-term corticosteroids.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Original Purple Rice Yoghurt is a Singaporean composite dish consisting of tangy fermented yoghurt folded together with cooked chewy purple rice (black glutinous rice, also known as 紫米 in Chinese). The purple rice component is a whole-grain variety rich in anthocyanins — the same class of polyphenol antioxidants found in blueberries and red cabbage — which give the dish its distinctive deep purple colour and contribute anti-inflammatory properties. The yoghurt component provides live bacterial cultures, calcium, and protein, while the purple rice contributes complex carbohydrates, dietary fibre (3.7 g/100g), iron (2.55 mg/100g), magnesium (40 mg/100g), and potassium (327 mg/100g). The texture is a contrast of smooth, creamy yoghurt and chewy, slightly sticky rice grains, producing the characteristic 'QQ' mouthfeel prized in Singaporean and Chinese food culture. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, and 0.21 g sugar. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 50 (low), making it a more glycaemically moderate grain-dairy dish compared to white rice preparations. Sodium is 231 mg/100g, which is borderline; however, the potassium-to-sodium ratio is favourable at approximately 1.4:1. The dish is suitable as a breakfast, snack, or light meal and is commonly consumed in Singapore. It is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-compatible. It is not suitable for vegans or those with dairy allergies.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010445, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean food records. All core macros and key micronutrients are present. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature, clearly documented. Multilingual names are provided for all four mandatory Singaporean languages (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) in addition to English.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Original Purple Rice Yoghurt is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010445) as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 1.8 g/100g from the yoghurt component based on USDA FoodData Central plain yoghurt data, but this estimate is not entered as a numeric value to preserve source integrity; it is noted here for clinical reference only. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this food item as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database entry G00010445; yoghurt-based dishes with no egg or meat components typically report negligible to zero cholesterol at this formulation. 4. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for black glutinous rice (GI approximately 42–55) and plain yoghurt (GI approximately 14–36); no direct GI measurement for this composite product was found in approved sources. 5. Halal status is assumed based on ingredients (yoghurt and purple rice); formal halal certification should be verified against the product label. 6. The food_group 'Grains and staples' and food_subgroup 'Rice-based dishes' are preserved from the input HPB record, though the dish has a significant dairy component; this classification reflects the dominant carbohydrate source.
